question: Why do we test software? What is the goal of testing?
answer:
Testing finds defects before users do, reduces risk, and builds confidence that the product meets requirements. The goal is not to prove the software is perfect — it is to provide information about quality so the team can decide whether to ship.

  • Catch bugs early (cheaper to fix).
  • Verify behaviour against requirements.
  • Prevent regressions when code changes.
  • Improve trust for release decisions.
tip: Say: testing reduces risk and informs release decisions — it does not guarantee zero bugs.
deep:
Beginner framing

Think of testing like checking a car before a long trip: you are not trying to invent a perfect car in the garage — you are finding what is broken or risky before customers drive it.

What testing is (and is not)

  • Is: a process of evaluating software against expected behaviour and risk.
  • Is not: a promise that every bug is found. Exhaustive testing of real systems is impossible.

Why interviews love this

Junior answers stop at "to find bugs". Stronger answers add: cost of late defects, regression safety, and helping product/business decide release readiness.

How to say it in an interview

“We test to find defects early, confirm the product matches requirements, and give the team evidence to ship with acceptable risk. Testing never proves zero bugs — it reduces uncertainty.”

question: Verification vs Validation?
answer:
Verification asks: Are we building the product right? — checks against specs/design (reviews, static analysis, unit tests against requirements).

Validation asks: Are we building the right product? — checks whether it satisfies the real user need (UAT, usability, acceptance testing).
tip: Memory aid: Verification = specs; Validation = user/business need.
deep:
Simple example

  • Verification: requirement says checkout must apply a 10% coupon — your test confirms the discount math and API response.
  • Validation: users in UAT say the coupon field is confusing and they abandon checkout — product is "correct" but not the right experience.

Where each happens

VerificationValidation
QuestionBuilt correctly?Useful / acceptable?
AgainstRequirements, design, code standardsUser needs, business goals
ExamplesCode review, unit/API tests, walkthroughsUAT, beta, usability testing

How to say it in an interview

“Verification checks we implemented the spec correctly; validation checks we solved the real user problem. A feature can pass verification and still fail validation.”

question: Functional vs non-functional testing?
answer:
Functional checks what the system does — features, business rules, inputs/outputs (login works, order is created, invoice total is correct).

Non-functional checks how the system behaves — performance, security, usability, reliability, compatibility.
tip: Functional = correct behaviour; non-functional = quality attributes under real conditions.
deep:
Examples you can name

  • Functional: place order, apply coupon, cancel subscription, role-based access to a page.
  • Non-functional: page loads under 2s for 1k users, password storage is hashed, app works on Chrome/Firefox, system recovers after crash.

Why SDETs care

Many teams automate functional API/UI flows first. Non-functional work (load, security) often needs specialised tools and environments — mention that you know the difference even if your current role is mostly functional automation.

How to say it in an interview

“Functional testing verifies features against business rules. Non-functional testing verifies quality attributes like speed, security, and usability. Both are needed for release confidence.”

question: Explain the main test levels: unit, integration, system, acceptance.
answer:
• Unit — smallest piece of code (method/class) in isolation; fast; usually by developers.
  • Integration — modules/services working together (API + DB, service A calling service B).
  • System — the full application as a whole against requirements.
  • Acceptance (UAT) — business/users confirm it is ready for real use.

You need all levels; relying only on UI/system tests is slow and brittle.
tip: Map each bug to the lowest level that could have caught it — that is seniority.
deep:
Who usually owns what

LevelTypical ownerExample
UnitDeveloperDiscount calculator returns 10%
IntegrationDev / SDETOrder API writes a row in DB
System / E2ESDET / QAUser completes checkout in UI
AcceptanceBusiness / UAT usersClient signs off release

Common anti-pattern

Only E2E UI tests: suite is slow, flaky, and hard to debug. Better: many fast lower-level checks + fewer critical E2E paths.

How to say it in an interview

“Unit catches logic bugs cheaply, integration catches wiring bugs, system confirms the whole product, and acceptance confirms business readiness. As an SDET I automate heavily at API/UI system level, but I still push for unit coverage on complex rules.”

question: What is shift-left testing?
answer:
Shift-left means starting quality activities earlier in the lifecycle — during requirements, design, and development — instead of only testing at the end.

Examples: review requirements for testability, write acceptance criteria with the story, automate unit/API tests in the PR pipeline, pair with developers on edge cases.
tip: Finding a requirement bug in planning is cheaper than finding it in UAT.
deep:
Why it matters

Defect cost grows over time. A missing requirement found in production can mean hotfixes, data repair, and lost trust. Shift-left moves feedback closer to the change.

Concrete SDET behaviours

  • Ask clarifying questions in grooming ("What if payment fails mid-order?").
  • Add API tests for new endpoints in the same sprint.
  • Block merges when critical automated checks fail (CI).
  • Share bug patterns so developers prevent repeats.

How to say it in an interview

“Shift-left means I don’t wait for a ‘QA phase’. I get involved in requirements, automate early, and put fast checks in CI so defects are found when they are cheapest to fix.”

question: Positive testing vs negative testing?
answer:
Positive testing uses valid inputs and happy-path flows to confirm the system works as expected (correct login, successful order).

Negative testing uses invalid/unexpected inputs to confirm the system handles failure safely (wrong password, missing required field, SQL injection attempt).
tip: Interviewers expect both: prove it works, and prove it fails gracefully.
deep:
Examples

  • Positive: valid user + correct password → dashboard.
  • Negative: valid user + wrong password → clear error, no session created.
  • Negative: quantity = -1 or "abc" → validation error, no order placed.

Why negative tests catch expensive bugs

Many production incidents are edge cases: empty payloads, timeouts, duplicate submit clicks, expired tokens. Negative testing is how you design those scenarios on purpose.

How to say it in an interview

“Positive tests confirm the happy path. Negative tests confirm invalid input is rejected safely — with the right message, status code, and no bad side effects like partial orders.”
